text: Single Docking Robotic-Assisted Retrorectus Ventral Incisional Hernia Repair with MeSH. A seventy-six-year-old female presented to clinic with symptomatic epigastric ventral incisional hernia. Her complaints include nausea, amnesis, pain in the epigastric bulge, and difficulty with defecation when the bulge is present. Her past medical history is as listed below. and her surgical history is as follows. Her social history is significant for being a former smoker. She denies alcohol or illicit drug use, and her BMI is thirty.
Preoperative workup R as shown. Preoperative imaging using CAT scan of the abdomen and pelvis was performed. This showed an epigastric herniate with a fascial defect of four point five centimeters.

The patient is positioned supine on the operating table with both arms tucked. The robotic system is docked over the head, with the camera port placed midline above the pubic bone. Two robotic arms are docked in the left and right lower quadrants of the abdomen.

Following abdominal insufflation, the surgeon incises the peritoneum circumferentially around the hernia defect, entering the preperitoneal plane. Using a monopolar cautery hook, the surgeon dissects the preperitoneal space laterally beyond the rectus abdominis muscle until reaching the linea semilunaris, with clear visualization maintained throughout the procedure.

The surgeon mobilizes the right rectus muscle and excises the hernia sac at the midline. Attention is then shifted to the left side of the hernia, where the preperitoneal plane is entered. Instruments are exchanged between the robotic arms to facilitate left lateral dissection, extending to the linea semilunaris, with exceptional visualization of the surgical field.

The surgeon completes bilateral retro-rectus muscle release and re-approximates the midline fascia without tension using a unidirectional barbed suture. A biosynthetic mesh is placed in a sublay position for reinforcement and fixated to the rectus sheath with two interrupted sutures. The posterior sheath is closed with the same suture in a running fashion.

Postoperative incisions are as shown in this picture. Operative time was a total of one hundred twenty minutes with estimated blood loss of thirty milliliters. After the surgery, patient was hospitalized for pain control and to ensure hemodynamic stability. She was advanced to a cardiac diet on postoperative day one and discharged home the morning of postoperative day two.
Her two-week follow-up revealed no further need for pain medications, good tolerance of her diet, normal bowel and urinary functions, and complete resolution of her hernia symptoms.
